coredata.dc:title: Synergistic role of N-doped carbon quantum dots on Zn-doped cobalt ferrite (N-CQDs/ZnCF) for the enhanced photodegradation of oxytetracycline under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Mmelesi, Olga K.
Ammar-Merah, Souad
Nkambule, Thabo T.I.
Kefeni, Kebede K.
Kuvarega, Alex T.
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots/Zn0.4Co0.6Fe2O4 (N-CQDs/ZnCF) composite was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method.XRD, HR-TEM, UV–Vis/DRS,PL, andXPSwere used to characterize the synthesized nanoparticles. The band gap energies of the synthesized nanomaterials were found to be less than 2 eV. PL results confirmed improved separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers on the N-CQDs/ZnCF. Additionally, HR-TEM images showed quasi-spherical and cubic morphology for both N-CQDs and ZnCF, respectively. The XPS survey spectra of 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF confirmed the presence of Zn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, N, O, and C on surfaces of N-CQDs/ZnCF. The pristine NPsand N-CQDs/ZnCF NCswere tested for the photodegradation of oxytetracycline, under visible light irradiation. The 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF nanocomposite showed the highest photocatalytic activity of up to 98% in 100 min. This enhancement was attributed to the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and ZnCF in the heterojunction, which promotes charge carrier separation and boosts photoactivity in degrading OTC.

head.ce:title: Synergistic role of N-doped carbon quantum dots on Zn-doped cobalt ferrite (N-CQDs/ZnCF) for the enhanced photodegradation of oxytetracycline under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots/Zn0.4Co0.6Fe2O4 (N-CQDs/ZnCF) composite was successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method.XRD, HR-TEM, UV–Vis/DRS,PL, andXPSwere used to characterize the synthesized nanoparticles. The band gap energies of the synthesized nanomaterials were found to be less than 2 eV. PL results confirmed improved separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers on the N-CQDs/ZnCF. Additionally, HR-TEM images showed quasi-spherical and cubic morphology for both N-CQDs and ZnCF, respectively. The XPS survey spectra of 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF confirmed the presence of Zn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, N, O, and C on surfaces of N-CQDs/ZnCF. The pristine NPsand N-CQDs/ZnCF NCswere tested for the photodegradation of oxytetracycline, under visible light irradiation. The 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF nanocomposite showed the highest photocatalytic activity of up to 98% in 100 min. This enhancement was attributed to the synergistic effect of N-CQDs and ZnCF in the heterojunction, which promotes charge carrier separation and boosts photoactivity in degrading OTC.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution, particularly due to pharmaceutical contamination, has received significant attention in recent years because of the continued global economic and social growth [1]. Oxytetracycline (OTC), a tetracycline (TC) antibiotic, is a pharmaceutical chemical that is widely used in human and veterinary medicine, and as a cattle growth stimulant [2]. OTC is reported in quantities of μg/L to mg/L in the aquatic environment because of its persistent and recalcitrant characteristics, causing environmental problems such as bacterial antibiotic resistance and ecotoxicity [3]. Current wastewater treatment technologies cannot completely remove OTC from the aquatic environment, hence it is critical to identify alternative treatment technologies [4]. Photocatalysis has emerged and advanced in the field of wastewater treatment in recent years due to its high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, versatility, and ease of application [5,6]. Semiconductor photocatalysts have received a lot of attention and significant progress has been made in the realm of environmental protection [7–9]. TiO2, one of the most common semiconductor photocatalysts has several advantages, including good photostability, environmental friendliness, high chemical resistance, low cost, and so on [10,11]. However, TiO2 has a significant disadvantage of a large band gap (3.2 eV) which limits its capacity to harvest visible light for photoactivity. Another challenge is its recovery from the slurry solution after use [12]. These challenges have prompted more research efforts aimed at finding alternative semiconductors with more large-scale applicability properties [6].

When compared to other soft ferrites, zinc-doped cobalt ferrites (ZnCoFe2O4) have captivated the research community with significant features such as high saturation magnetization and permeability [13,14]. Owing to their unique properties and applications, investigations into ZnCoFe2O4 has become a topical area of study [15–17]. ZnCoFe2O4 nanocrystals are a type of soft-natured magnetic material with a restricted saturation magnetization, high magneto-crystallinity, intrinsic coercivity, and anisotropy constant [18]. These fascinating properties are influenced by several factors, including the synthesis technique and dopant concentration. Although electrons are rapidly excited in spinel ferrites when exposed to light radiation, rapid electron-hole pair recombination results in poor photocatalytic efficiency in spinel ferrites [19]. In a study by Sundararajan and co-workers, 95% degradation efficiency of Rhodamine B on Co0.6Zn0.4Fe2O4 was achieved under visible light irradiation after 3.5 h using hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant. The high efficacy was as attributed to the low electron-hole pair recombination of Co0.6Zn0.4Fe2O4, which allowed more time for charge carriers to be available for the degradation of rhodamine B in solution and the first order rate constant k1 for Co0.6Zn0.4Fe2O4 is shown to be 2.6 times faster than the pristine CoFe2O4 [20]. However, zinc-doped cobalt ferrite in the absence of an oxidant, exhibits moderate photocatalytic efficacy, hence the need to couple with other photocatalyst such as carbon quantum dots to form an effective heterojunction.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are well known for their chemical stability and sensitivity to visible light, as well as their unique photoluminescence (PL) upconversion feature and non-toxicity [21–23]. While CQDs offer certain appealing properties, they also show several drawbacks, such as difficult separation methods, purification and functionalization, low quantum yields, and ambiguity in their geometry, composition, and structure [24,25]. As a result, research to improve the fluorescence properties of CQDs through surface passivation and heteroatom doping is still ongoing. Heteroatoms such as nitrogen [26], phosphorus [27], boron [28], and sulphur [29,30], have been widely employed to strengthen the emission properties of CQDs. Among the heteroatoms, the nitrogen (N) atom is the first and most documented, as well as the most effective for visible light response [31–33]. Nitrogen is a common dopant with five valence electrons and an atomic size comparable to carbon, therefore nitrogen doping is the most widely used method for improving the PL properties of CQDs. The nitrogen atom injects electrons into CQDs, altering the internal electronic environment and thus improving their fluorescence properties [34]. CQDs have recently received a lot of attention for photoinduced processes and integration into nanocomposite photocatalysts. This is due to their unique optical and electronic properties such as their up-conversion photoluminescence, strong light absorption, tunable bandgap, photoexcited electron transfer ability, and inhibition of electron-hole pair recombination [30,35]. In a report by Duo et al., N-Carbon dots/BiOBr composites demonstrated higher photocatalytic activity than pure N-CDs or BiOBr, due to the upconversion effect of the N-CDs [36]. The incorporation of CQDs on other photocatalysts can enhance photocatalytic activity while allowing for the absorption of longer wavelength light for activation. CQDs can generate more electron-hole pairs and reduce electron-hole pair recombination, which is a requirement for enhanced photocatalytic activity [37]. Additionally, Huang et al. observed that ZnFe2O4/CQDs nanocomposite exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity (38%) towards NO removal in 30 min. This was attributed to CQDs acting as electron traps for electron transfer during photocatalysis [38]. Therefore, the integration of CQDs with metal oxide photocatalysts for the degradation of pharmaceutical contaminants offers potential for future water purification.

In this work, N-CQDs/Zn0.4Co0.6Fe2O4 nanocomposites (N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs) were synthesized for photocatalytic degradation of OTC. This study intended to prove and authenticate the enhanced performance of N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs for the degradation of pharmaceuticals by designing a magnetic, visible light active heterojunction. The morphology, structure, and properties of the N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs nanocomposites were evaluated by various characterization techniques. Finally, the potential photocatalytic mechanism of the Zn0.4Co0.6Fe2O4/N-CQDs was proposed.

Cobalt nitrate, hexahydrate (≥99.0%), iron nitrate nonahydrate (≥98.0%), sodium hydroxide, oxytetracycline, isopropanol, ascorbic acid (≥99.5%), formic acid (≥95%), humic acid, sodium phosphate (≥99%), sodium nitrate decahydrate (≥98.5%), sodium chloride (≥99%), sodium carbonate decahydrate (≥99%), urea (≥99%), citric acid monohydrate (≥99%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (South Africa). Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (≥99.99%) was purchased from CC Immelmann. All reagents were used without further purification. De-ionized water was used for preparing all the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zinc doped cobalt ferrite nanoparticles (NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: Zinc-doped cobalt ferrite with a general formula Zn0.4Co0,6Fe2O4 (ZnCF) was synthesized using a co-precipitation method. In 100 mL of distilled water, a mole ratio of 1:2 was used for Zn(NO3)2·6H2O/Co(NO3)2·6H2O and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O Following that, 2 M NaOH was added dropwise into the metal precursor solution until the pH reached 11 and stirred for 2 h at 80 °C until a precipitate formed. Before being dried overnight at 100 °C, the dark brown/black precipitates were washed three times with distilled water and ethanol. The NPs were then calcined for 3 h at 500 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C /min. The synthesized samples were then named CF and ZnCF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped quantum carbon dots were synthesized by the microwave method. In a typical synthesis, a 1:1 wt ratio of citric acid and urea was mixed with 10 mL of water. Then the solution was stirred for 15 min at room temperature and heated in a domestic microwave at a microwave power of 594 W for 5 min to yield a black residue. The mixture underwent polymerization, carbonization, and the creation of carbon dots while being heated. The synthesized N-CQDs were cooled to room temperature, ground to a fine powder, and stored in a dry cabinet for further use. The frequency and total microwave power output of the domestic microwave used for the synthesis are 2.45 GHz and 900 W.

The N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs with N-CQDs ratios of 0.25, 5, 10, and 25%, were synthesized using the hydrothermal method. ZnCF and N-CQDs, with appropriate ratios, were sonicated in 100 mL of water for 30 min. The mixture was then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the particles were separated from the solution using a magnet and further centrifuged to remove the excess water. The particles were then dried overnight in an oven at 80 °C. The samples were named N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs with their respective weight %.

A Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray Diffractometer was used to evaluate the phase structure in the 2θ range of 20–60°. Using PerkinElmer Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) frontier, the nanomaterials were analyzed in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 using the KBr method. The diffuse reflectance UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a PerkinElmer UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer 6505, from 250 to 800 nm. Through the Tauc plots, bandgap energies of the nanomaterials were estimated. Horiba Fluorolog-3 Jobnyvon spectrophotometer was used to analyze the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the NPs and NCs, at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The thermal stability of the NCs were evaluated using TGA 5500 Discovery series (TA instruments), under nitrogen and at a ramp rate of 10. °C/min. The morphology of the N-CQDs/ZnCF NC-10% were studied at 200 kV using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy- HRTEM (JEOL JEM 2100 Plus and a JEOL JEM 2010 UHR) microscopes. The surface charge of the photocatalyst was analyzed by Nano zetasizer (Malvern, Worcestershire, UK). The surface chemical oxidation state of the NCs were studied employing X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) on a VG ESCALAB MARK II (VG, UK).

The photodegradation experiments were carried out on a custom-made bench-scale photoreactor consisting of a 0.5 L UV glass reactor with connections, a quartz jacketed tube reactor to hold the lamp, a 4 L tank for cooling, a magnetic stirrer, and a 250 W HPMVL visible light lamp. In a typical experiment, in the photoreactor, 0.2 g/L of the catalyst was introduced to 100 mL (10 mg/L) of OTC. To establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium, the slurry solution was agitated at 650 rpm for 20 min, then the light was switched on. 2 mL aliquots of the OTC mixture were sampled at 20 min intervals and filtered using a syringe filter (0.45 µm) for analysis. The effect of factors such as pH, initial concentration of OTC, and radical scavengers was investigated. UV–Vis spectrometer and TOC analyzer (Teledyne Tekmar TN, USA) were used to monitor the photodegradation of OTC. The degradation efficiency of OTC was determined using the equation below.
(1)

where k and t are observed rate constant and time respectively. Humic acid (HA), and anionic sodium salts salt were introduced to the reaction solution to assess the consequences of the presence of other water pollutants on the photodegradation of OTC under a similar experimental environment. The catalyst was also recycled and reused for four cycles to evaluate its stability on reusability.

The reactive oxygen species responsible for the photodegradation processes were identified using radical scavengers. To distinguish the roles of •OH., •O2 −, and h+ in the degradation of OTC, radical probes isopropanol (ISO), ascorbic acid (AA), and formic acid (FA) were used. The concentration of every scavenger was held constant at 10 mM.

XRD was used to determine the phase and crystalline structure of the produced N-CQDs, cobalt ferrite (CF) Zn doped cobalt ferrite (ZnCF), and N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs, as illustrated in Fig. 1 a. At 25°, the N-CQDs show an amorphous-crystalline pattern, which matches what has been found in the literature [39]. Nevertheless, the characteristics XRD pattern of CF and ZnCF NPs matched the JCPDS NO: 01-074-6403, confirming the spinel with a cubic structure [40]. The peaks of ZnCF and CF nanoparticles (NPs) were comparable, indicating that doping had no effect on the structure of the CF NPs [41]. Additionally, N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs (0.25, 5, 10, and 25%), showed the characteristic peaks of spinel ferrites. The trace peak of N-CQDs, on the other hand, was not observed due to the low concentration and, high and uniform dispersion of N-CQDs [42]. There was no shift in the peak position of the NCs, implying that the addition of N-CQDs to ZnCF NPs had no effect on the phase structure of the spinel. Fig. 1b shows the FTIR spectra of N-CQDs, CF, ZnCF NPs, and N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs (0.25, 5, 10, and 25%). On the edges and basal plane of the N-CQDs, oxygen-containing functional groups such as COOH, OH, COC, and CO were introduced during polymerization and carbonization [43]. As a result, the synthesized N-CQDs were extremely soluble in hydrophilic solvents. The distinctive absorption peaks of NH (3410 cm−1) and the aromatic CN (1420 cm−1), were also observed, demonstrating that the CQDs matrix was doped with nitrogen [39,44]. For CF and ZnCF NPs, two main peaks of interest were observed at 561 and 446 cm−1, which corresponds to M2+-O in the octahedral and Fe3+ O on the tetrahedral positions, respectively [45]. After the formation of the NCs, the CO group slightly shifted, because the carboxylic groups of the N-CQDs were transformed to carboxylate ions [45].

The synthesized NPs and NCs were analyzed by UV–Vis-DRS, to evaluate the optical absorption capacity (Fig. 2 a). All the samples exhibited wide absorption capacity ranging from ∼250 to 800 nm. The addition of N-CQDs increased the absorption range of the composites in both the UV and visible light regions. This observation suggests that CQDs enhance visible light absorption, which is vital for the generation of more electron-hole pairs for photocatalytic reactions. Tauc plots were used to determine the energy band gaps of the synthesized materials (Fig. S1 and Table S1). All the bad gap energies were estimated to be <2 eV confirming that the nanocomposites could be activated through visible light irradiation. Also, the synthesized N-CQDs showed a green fluorescence under UV light (Fig. S2).

The PL spectra of the synthesized N-CQDs in Fig. S3 showed that the quantum dots are excitation wavelength dependent. The charge migration and extent of electron-hole pair recombination in the photocatalysts were determined using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Fig. 2b) at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The emission peak appeared at 470 nm. It can be observed that the emission of photons for N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs, subsequent from the recombination of electron-hole pair provides the photoluminescence spectra with lower PL intensity, which implies the reduced charge carrier recombination [46]. The PL intensity was reduced remarkably by integrating N-CQDs into ZnCF. This confirms the enhanced lifetime of the charge carriers and thus the electrons generated can be transferred to N-CQDs from the surface of ZnCF [45]. As a result, charge carrier recombination is significantly reduced, resulting in improved photocatalytic activity.

TGA under an inert environment was used to demonstrate the thermal stability of synthesized nanomaterials (Fig. 3 ). The initial weight loss (9%) was observed at around 200 °C, which can be attributed to the desorption of water molecules of physically adsorbed water molecules, as well as condensation or removal of OH groups on the surface of the N-CQDs. Between 200 and 550 °C, the second phase of weight loss (36%) was observed, which corresponds to the disintegration of stable functional groups such as COOH groups at the periphery of the N-CQDs structure. The massive weight loss of 55% beyond 550 °C is attributed to the loss of a considerable number of functional groups on the N-CQDs and the decomposition of the N-CQDs (carbon) skeleton [31]. For CF and ZnCF NPs the TGA, the curve indicates weight loss at 200 °C, similar to the N-CQDs. Furthermore, two thermal decomposition steps occurred. In the first step, at around 580 °C, there was a weight loss of 2% and 3% respectively, due to the removal of the hydroxyl group, moisture, carbon, etc.,[47] and up to 900 °C, weight loss was essentially non-existent in the second stage [15]. However, CF was more thermally stable compared to ZnCF, and this can be attributed to doping CF with Zn, which results in the lattice strain, hence reducing its thermal stability. The 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF NC showed a similar decomposition pattern with N-CQDs, though it was more thermally stable compared to N-CQDs, with weight loss of 8%. This observation acknowledges that the composite has approximately 10% of N-CQDs.

The morphologies and microstructures of the 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs were investigated using HR-TEM, SEM, and EDS (Fig. 4 ). The structure of the nanocomposite in Fig. 4a showed quasi-spherical and cubic morphology for both N-CQDs and ZnCF, respectively. These images showed that the CQDs adhered to the surface of ZnCF and were well dispersed. The spinel structure predicted by XRD data was confirmed by lattice fringes with high intensities of the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes, and d-spacing for the (220) plane in good agreement with XRD results. SAED patterns show seamless lattice planes and bright spots, confirming the crystallinity and poly-crystalline nature of the N-CQDs/ZnCF nanocomposite (Fig. 4b). The SEM image reveals that the synthesized NCs were agglomerated and dispersed on large crystalline blocks as small aggregated lumps (Fig. 4c). In addition, the EDS pattern confirmed the presence of Co, Zn, Fe, O, N, and C in the sample, with Fe being the most prominent of all elements (Fig. 4d).

The chemical composition and chemical bonding of the nanocomposites was studied using XPS. The XPS survey spectra of ZnCF and 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF confirmed the presence of Zn2+, Co2+, Fe3+, and C on both surfaces of ZnCF and N-CQDs/ZnCF (Fig. 5 a). The C in the spectra of pure ZnCF is from the instrument calibration [48]. The stronger C1s and new N1s peak appeared in the spectra of N-CQDs/ZnCF composites confirming the presence of N-CQDs. The N-CQDs/ZnCF spectra exhibit Zn2p at binding energies of 1021 and 1044 eV, corresponding to Zn2p3/2 and Zn2p1/2 electrons, respectively, in the Zn2+ oxidation state (Fig. 5b) [38]. The primary peaks in the Co2p XPS spectra at binding energies of 794, 795, and 779 eV, corresponding to the Co2p1/2 and Co2p3/2 and spin–orbit peaks of the CoO phase, respectively. In general, the Fe2p states of the Fe atom can be applied to distinguish between Fe2+ and Fe3+. The peaks of Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 states on the ZnCF spectra can be observed in the range of 710–711 eV and 724–725 eV (Fig. 5c). The peak in the range of 710–711 eV is attributed to the Fe3+ cation sited at the octahedral site in the spinel structure. In the peak range of 724–725 eV, Fe2+ is confirmed at the tetrahedral site in the spinel structure. The XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 5e) was deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.6, 531.5, and 532.9 eV corresponding to lattice O2−, CO/NO, and CO groups, respectively. The deconvolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 5g) revealed five peaks, at 283.5 eV, 284.9, 286.2, 288.1, and 289.1 eV, corresponding to CC (sp2), CC (sp3), CO, CN/CO, and OCO, noticeably indicating that the synthesized N-CQDs consisted mainly of sp2 and sp3 carbons [49]. In the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 5f) two peaks at 398.2 and 408.6 eV can be assigned to pyridinic N and nitrate N, respectively, on the N-CQDs surface [1]. The XPS results verified that the synthesized N-CQDs/ZnCF were functionalized by OH, NH2, and COOH groups, which was further proved by EDS and FTIR analysis.

To assess the photodegradation activities of the prepared samples, a typical antibiotic, OTC, was chosen as the model pollutant. Fig. 6 a presents the UV–Vis profile of OTC degradation with time. The absorption peaks of OTC declined gradually with increasing irradiation time, suggesting that N-CQDs/ZnCF nanocomposite could degrade the OTC chromophores responsible for the absorption peaks. The blank experiment (Fig. 6b) confirmed that OTC is stable to photolysis. Adsorption of OTC on N-CQDs/ZnCF was minimal as only about 20% of the OTC was adsorbed after 100 mins. The degradation efficiency of N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs was higher than that of the CF, ZnCF, and N-CQDs, which were all higher than the photolysis and adsorption, respectively. In Fig. 6d, the photodegradation kinetics fitted to the first-order kinetic model. The rate constant for N-CQDs/ZnCF-10% was 0.0191 min−1, which was about 1.93 times that of ZnCF (0.0099 min−1) and 2.98 times that of N-CQDs (0.0064 min−1), respectively. The addition of N-CQDs improved the transfer rate of photogenerated electrons and inhibited the electron-hole pairs recombination, consequently resulting in enhanced photocatalytic degradation efficacy. However, much higher loadings of N-CQDs (20%) in the nanocomposite results in higher turbidity and light scattering which attenuates light transmittance, resulting in reduced photocatalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of water matrix and pH on OTC degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of other inorganic and organic pollutants such as inorganic ions and natural organic matter on the photodegradation of OTC were evaluated in a simulated real wastewater matrix [50]. Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2− and CO3 2− were introduced into the reaction solutions (OTC) as representative inorganic anions to investigate their potential effects on OTC removal. As shown in Fig. 7 a, 5 mM of Cl−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, SO4 2− and CO3 2− all showed a negative impact on OTC degradation, reducing the efficiency by 11.98, 17.20, 9.56, 19.51, and 24.43%, respectively. This can be attributed to the anions interacting with the photocatalyst, thus occupying some of the active sites and limiting the access of OTC to the surface of the catalyst [51]. However, CO3 2− anions, drastically reduced the OTC degradation efficiency. This is attributed to CO3 2− ions acting as a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals, producing carbonate radicals during the reaction (rate: 8.5 × 106 M−1s−1), as shown by equation (3).
(3) HO• + HCO3 − → CO3 •− + H2O

Humic acid (HA) was also used to assess the impact of natural organic matter (NOM) on the photocatalytic oxidation process [52]. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, HA inhibited OTC degradation and acted as a radical quencher, competing for radicals with OTC. Since HA can be adsorbed onto the catalyst surface and block reactive sites, it can be postulated to have an inhibiting effect [53]. Therefore, the presence of background constituents in water may reduce the efficiency of advanced oxidation processes. In real freshwater, the pH ranges between 6 and 9, while groundwater is weakly acidic (pH between 5.0 and 6.5). As a result, the effect of pH (3–9) on OTC photodegradation efficiency was also investigated and results are presented in Fig. 7c and d. The photodegradation efficiency and reaction rate constant of OTC both increased as the pH was increased from 3.5 to 6.7. At lower pH, the concentration of hydrogen ion (H+) can react with . O2 to form a weak oxidant H2O2, resulting in a decrease in photodegradation ability in an acidic solution [40]. Additionally, the NCs was found to have a negatively charged (−9.11 mV) surface, hence further increase in pH value to 9.3 the photodegradation efficacy decreases. This was attributed to the charge repulsion of the photocatalyst and the pollutant because OTC at pH of 9.3 has a negative charge OTC2− [8].

The reusability of the nanocomposite catalyst was investigated after consecutive experimental runs. There was a negligible drop in efficacy after four repeated cycles (97%, 94%, 92%, and 89%) The decrease in total organic carbon (TOC) was measured to evaluate the extent of mineralization of OTC. It was observed that after four cycles TOC removal was around 76% from an initial value of about 90% (Fig. 8 a). The decline can be attributed to the loss of catalyst during the recovery process. The TOC degradation efficiency was lower than the UV–Vis efficiency, this could imply that OTC was degraded to small organic molecules. The FTIR and XRD analysis of fresh and spent photocatalyst showed no distinct differences, indicating that the photocatalyst remained stable after four cycles (Fig. 8b and c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of reactive oxygen species and degradation mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To further unravel the role of the reactive oxygen species on OTC removal over 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF, the effects of various radical scavengers such as ascorbic acid (AA), formic acid (FA), and isopropanol (ISO), were examined. As demonstrated in Fig. 9 , all three radical scavengers resulted in reduced photocatalytic degradation of OTC. These results further confirm that •O2 − radicals made a significant contribution to OTC removal over N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs and therefore, the reaction mechanism of the 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF photocatalyst was proposed (Fig. 10 ).

Furthermore, the conduction band edge positions (ECB and EVB) were calculated using equations (4) and (5)
(4)
E
CB
= χ -
E C
-
1 2
E g
(5)
E
VB
=
E
CB
+
E g
where, χ is the absolute electronegativity of the nanomaterial, EC is the energy of free electrons on the normal hydrogen scale, NHE (∼4.5 eV), and Eg is the energy band gap of the NPs [54].

Under visible light irradiation, the electrons are excited from the VB to the CB of ZnCF, leaving holes in the VB. In the absence of N-CQDs, most electron–hole pairs quickly recombine and only a small fraction of electrons can be trapped by O2 to generate •O2 − or are trapped by Fe3+. Considering that the CB potential of ZnCF (0.41 eV) was lower than the standard reduction potential of O2/O2 ·− (−0.33 eV), the electrons on the CB of ZnCF would not be able to reduce O2 to generate O2 ·−. In the presence of N-CQDs, the photogenerated electrons on the CB of ZnCF can be transferred to the N-CQDs, due to their excellent electronic conductivity [43]. The transferred electrons accumulated on the N-CQDs can then be captured by the adsorbed O2 to form superoxide radicals (•O2 −) [55]. The excellent adsorptive property of N-CQDs due to the π-π interaction between conjugated structure of CQDs and OTC [56], ensures enhanced adsorption of OTC on the photocatalyst surface for enhanced degradation. Subsequently, OTC can be further oxidized by·•O2 − or •OH on its surface. It can be envisaged that the •O2 − radicals play a significant role during photocatalysis. Furthermore, photogenerated holes also contributed to OTC oxidation in this system because h+ in VB of ZnCF can directly oxidize the OTC [40,57]. The introduction of ISO does not yield much suppression of the degradation efficiency of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrical energy consumption of the proposed photodegradation system
ce:section.ce:para:
Determining the energy consumption of photodegradation systems is important on the current process design for practical water treatment applications. To propose oxidation systems based on the consumption of electricity to treat real wastewater, the energy consumption must be calculated. For this purpose, energy consumption (kWh/m3) was calculated using the IUPAC figure of merit and Eq. (6) [58,59].
(6)
EC = p t / l o g
c t
c 0
× V
where P is the electrical power consumed by the visible lamp (kW), t is the time required to degrade the OTC (hrs.), V is the volume used (L), C0 is the initial concentration (mg/L) of OTC and Ct is the final OTC concentration (mg/ L).

The EC of the synthesized photocatalysts ranged between 4.060 and 13.9492 kWh/m3 (Fig. 11 ), which was lower than the energy consumption reported by Rajiv et al., [59]. The low energy consumption for the 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF NCs can be attributed to the formation of a heterojunction through a proper coupling of the two photocatalysts to enhance the adsorption of pollutants, reduce electron-hole recombination, and produce more reactive species. In South Africa, the cost of electricity per kWh is $0.157, thus the electrical energy cost for degradation of OTC using 10% N-CQDs/ZnCF- NCs is estimated to be $0.639 per m−3 for the removal of 10 mg/Lof OTC.

In conclusion, ZnCF/N-CDQs nanocomposites were successfully fabricated via a facile hydrothermal process. Physico-chemical characterization confirmed the attachment of CQDs on the surface of ZnCF and the particles were quasi-spherical and cubic morphology for both N-CQDs and ZnCF. Significantly, compared with pure ZnCF, N-CQDs particles, ZnCF/N-CDQs hybrid materials displayed enhanced photocatalytic activity for OTC degradation under visible light. The 10% N-CDQs/ZnCF hybrid material exhibited the optimal photocatalytic performance of 98%. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was ascribed to the up converted PL and electron reservoir properties of N-CQDs. Trapping experiments confirmed •O2 −, and h+ as the main reactive species in OTC degradation. The nanocomposite showed high stability, with degradation efficiency of 86.60% after four consecutive reaction cycles expressed the nature of the excellent recyclability of this nanocomposite. The energy consumption for the best NC was 4.064 kWh/m3 and was 93 times lower than the conventional wastewater treatment (380 kWh/m3) in South Africa. This study shed light on engineering of N-CDQs/ZnCF/heterojunctions with enhanced photoactivity for visible light utilization in degradation of pharmaceutical in water.
